key_insight: The paper extends compatible-strain mixed finite elements from simplicial to general quadrilateral meshes by building physically compatible shape functions directly in the physical domain, enabling both compressible and incompressible 2D nonlinear elasticity.
engineering_relevance: This gives solid mechanics practitioners a more mesh-flexible and lower-DOF alternative for nonlinear elasticity simulations on quadrilateral grids, especially when higher-order accuracy is needed.
method: Use first-kind Nedelec interpolation for the displacement gradient to preserve compatibility.
Construct new stress shape functions in physical space via numerical integration, bypassing Piola-transformation limitations on general quads.
Add an incompressible variant with element-level pressure condensation to avoid extra global pressure unknowns.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical examples showing the quadrilateral elements can match problems that require second-order simplicial CSMFEs while using fewer degrees of freedom.
watch_item: Because the shape functions are computed directly in physical space, implementation is more involved and may be sensitive to quadrilateral geometry and numerical integration accuracy.
paper_title: First-Order Compatible-Strain Mixed Quadrilateral Finite Elements for 2D Nonlinear Elasticity
